error.signature: Session hijacking possible because session ID is not regenerated after login

verdict.summary: If the session ID remains the same after login, an attacker who knows the pre-auth session ID (e.g., via URL, XSS, or shared computer) automatically gains authenticated access. Most web frameworks do NOT auto-regenerate session IDs on login.

dead_ends.action: Assume the web framework regenerates session ID on login automatically
dead_ends.why_fails: Most frameworks (Express, Flask, Django, Spring) do NOT auto-regenerate session IDs on authentication state change. You must do it explicitly.

dead_ends.action: Create a new session only when the user first visits
dead_ends.why_fails: The issue isn't session creation timing but that the same session ID survives the authentication boundary. Pre-auth and post-auth must have different IDs.

workarounds.action: Explicitly regenerate session ID after successful authentication
workarounds.how: Flask: session.regenerate(); Django: request.session.cycle_key(); Express: req.session.regenerate(cb)

workarounds.action: Invalidate old session and create entirely new one on login
workarounds.how: Destroy existing session completely, create new session with new ID, then populate with user data

workarounds.action: Set session cookies with Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite=Lax attributes
